name: Unprotected Admin / Sensitive Functionality
description: Admin or sensitive endpoints are accessible without proper authentication because the server relies on URL obscurity rather than server-side authorization. The path may be discovered via robots.txt, client-side JavaScript, or directory guessing.
real_world_examples: Real-world example: robots.txt Disallow: /administrator-panel. Navigate directly → admin panel loads with no auth check.
Real-world example: JavaScript in the homepage source contains the admin panel URL. Inspecting source reveals it; direct navigation succeeds.
indicators: robots.txt or sitemap.xml reveals admin/management paths (Disallow: /admin-panel)
JavaScript source contains strings like adminPanelTag, isAdmin, /administrator-panel
/admin, /management, /console respond 200 to non-admin users
Response headers reveal framework with default admin panel location
technique: 1. Request /robots.txt — check Disallow entries for admin paths
2. View page source of the home page — search for 'admin', 'panel', 'secret', 'hidden' in JavaScript
3. Access discovered path directly with a low-privilege or anonymous session
4. Try standard paths: /admin, /admin/, /admin/dashboard, /administrator, /administrator-panel
5. If redirected (302), capture the response body before redirect — may contain admin HTML
success_condition: Low-privilege or anonymous actor receives 200 on an admin endpoint and the response body contains privileged data (user list, config, admin controls).

name: Parameter-Based Access Control (Privilege Escalation via Cookie/Hidden Field)
description: The server trusts a client-supplied role/privilege indicator (cookie, hidden field, URL parameter) rather than deriving the user's role from the authenticated session server-side. Modifying Admin=false to Admin=true grants full admin access.
real_world_example: Real-world example: After login, response sets cookie Admin=false. Intercept response and change to Admin=true. Reload /admin — full admin panel accessible.
indicators: Cookie contains plaintext role: Admin=false, role=user, isAdmin=false, access_level=1
URL parameter encodes role: ?admin=false, ?role=user
Hidden form field carries privilege level
Response body reveals current role value that matches a cookie name
technique: 1. Log in; inspect all cookies and response headers
2. Look for cookies/params with role semantics: Admin, isAdmin, role, access, privilege
3. INTERCEPT the server's login response and modify: Admin=false → Admin=true
4. Alternatively modify the cookie directly in the next request
5. Access /admin — if it returns 200 with admin content, cookie tamper succeeded
6. ALSO try: add &admin=true or &role=administrator as URL parameters
variations: JWT payload: change role claim (when signature not verified)
success_condition: After modifying the role parameter, /admin or equivalent returns 200 with admin content that was previously inaccessible.

name: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)
description: An authenticated user can access or modify another user's resources by substituting a predictable identifier (numeric user_id, username, GUID, sequential filename) in a request. The server does not verify that the requesting user owns the referenced object.
real_world_examples: Real-world example: /my-account?id=wiener → change id=carlos to get Carlos's API key.
Real-world example: Carlos has a blog post — his author profile link exposes his GUID. Use it in /my-account?id=<GUID> to access his account.
Real-world example: /my-account?id=administrator exposes admin password in a masked input field in the HTML source.
Real-world example: /download-transcript/1.txt — change to 2.txt to get other users' chat logs. Sequential filenames are the object reference.
Real-world example: change id=carlos — server issues a 302 redirect but the BODY of the redirect response still contains Carlos's API key.
indicators: User account page URL contains the username or id: ?id=wiener, /users/5
Download or transcript endpoint uses sequential numbers: /transcript/1.txt
Blog post author links contain a user GUID in the URL
API response includes owner fields: userId, email, apiKey belonging to the caller
technique: 1. Identify any endpoint that takes a user/object identifier in URL or body
2. Substitute another known username (carlos) or increment integer IDs (id=1, id=2...)
3. For GUIDs: find the target user's GUID from public content (blog posts, comments, author pages)
4. Check /my-account?id=administrator to expose admin credentials
5. For file references: enumerate /transcript/1.txt, 2.txt, 3.txt
6. IMPORTANT: always read the full response body on redirects (302) — data may leak before redirect fires
variations: String ID: id=carlos (not just integer IDs)
GUID from public blog/forum author links
Data in redirect body — server returns sensitive data before the 302 redirect executes
success_condition: Attacker receives 2xx (or 302 with data in body) containing victim's private data — API key, email, password, file contents — that belongs to a different user.

name: HTTP Method Override / Header-Based URL Bypass
description: Two sub-patterns: (A) The back-end framework respects X-Original-URL or X-Rewrite-URL headers, allowing bypass of front-end path-based access controls. (B) The server respects X-HTTP-Method-Override, allowing dangerous verbs (DELETE) disguised as safe ones (GET/POST).
real_world_example: Real-world example: front-end blocks GET /admin. Sending GET / with header X-Original-URL: /admin makes the back-end process /admin while the front-end sees /. Add ?username=carlos to query string and X-Original-URL: /admin/delete to delete the user.
indicators: Front-end returns 403 for /admin but back-end may still process it
Application uses a proxy/WAF in front of an app server (common in Java/Ruby/Python frameworks)
Swagger spec lists DELETE endpoints that return 403 when called directly
X-Original-URL or X-Rewrite-URL accepted (test by setting to /invalid and checking for 404 vs 403)
technique_url_bypass: 1. Send GET / with header X-Original-URL: /invalid — if 404 returned, header is processed
2. If confirmed, send GET / with X-Original-URL: /admin to reach admin panel
3. For actions requiring path params: add to query string e.g. ?username=carlos
4. Set X-Original-URL: /admin/delete to perform the action
technique_method_override: 1. Add header X-HTTP-Method-Override: DELETE to a POST request
3. Check whether the server performs the DELETE action
success_condition: Admin panel is accessible or privileged action is performed despite front-end blocking; user deletion or config change is reflected in the application state.

name: Function-Level Access Control Failure on Multi-Step Process
description: A multi-step privileged operation (e.g., promote user to admin) has an access control check on the FIRST step but NOT on the CONFIRMATION step. Any authenticated user who can replay the confirmation request can complete the privileged action.
real_world_example: Real-world example: admin promotes a user — GET /admin/roles?action=upgrade&username=carlos → confirmation POST /admin/roles with confirmed=true. An unprivileged user who captures the confirmation POST can replay it with their own session cookie to promote themselves.
indicators: Admin panel has a two-step action: 'Promote user' → confirmation page → confirmed
Confirmation step is a POST request with a predictable body (confirmed=true, action=upgrade)
Admin session cookie is the ONLY difference between admin and user confirmation requests
technique: 1. Log in as admin; perform the multi-step action and capture ALL requests
2. Identify the confirmation request (POST with confirmed=true or similar)
3. Send the confirmation request again with the UNPRIVILEGED user's session cookie
4. Change the username/target parameter if needed
5. If 200 or 302 returned, access control is missing on the confirmation step
success_condition: Low-privilege user successfully performs an admin action (role promotion, user deletion) by replaying the confirmation request with their own session.

name: Forced Browsing / Missing Function-Level Access Control
description: Pages or API routes for privileged functions exist but are not linked in the UI for low-privilege users. The server does not enforce authorization, so any user who discovers the URL can access it. NOTE: standard user account pages (/my-account, /profile, /settings) are NOT this pattern — they require login but are expected to exist.
indicators: JavaScript reveals conditional UI for admin users (feature flags, isAdmin checks with hidden URLs)
Guessable paths respond to low-priv users: /admin/users, /internal/config, /api/admin/*
Directory listing exposed via /static/ or /uploads/
Swagger/OpenAPI spec reveals endpoints not shown in UI
robots.txt Disallow entries for non-public paths
technique: 1. Extract all paths from JS bundles (grep for /admin, /internal, /manage, /staff)
2. Check robots.txt, sitemap.xml for disallowed paths
3. Access each discovered path with a low-privilege session
4. A [probed] endpoint that returns 302→login when anon but 200 when logged in is BAC-06 only if it is an admin/privileged area
5. Check: if path contains /admin, /manage, /staff, /internal, /console, /panel → BAC-06 candidate
success_condition: Low-privilege actor reaches a privileged endpoint (admin/management area) and receives useful response (200 with admin content, user list, configuration).

name: Email Domain Access Control Bypass (Inconsistent Security Controls)
description: The application grants elevated privileges based on the email domain (e.g., only @company.com employees get admin access). The vulnerability is that users can change their email AFTER registration without re-validation, allowing them to register with a legitimate email then switch to an arbitrary @company.com address.
real_world_example: Real-world example: admin access requires @dontwannacry.com email. Register with your legitimate email, confirm, then change email to anything@dontwannacry.com via account settings. Access /admin immediately — the domain check passes.
indicators: Error message when accessing /admin says 'Only @company.com employees can access this'
Registration requires email confirmation but email CHANGE does not
Account settings page allows email modification without re-confirming the new address
Access control based on email domain suffix rather than group membership
technique: 1. Attempt /admin access to confirm the required email domain from the error message
2. Register with a legitimate email and confirm account
3. Navigate to account settings; change email to arbitrary@<required-domain>.com
4. No email confirmation required for the domain change
5. Access /admin — domain check passes on the stored email
success_condition: After updating email to @required-domain.com without confirmation, /admin returns 200 with admin content.

name: Horizontal Privilege Escalation via Password Disclosure (IDOR + Credential Exposure)
description: The account management page renders the user's current password in a masked HTML input field (type=password). The password value is in the HTML source even though it appears hidden. Combined with IDOR (changing the id parameter), an attacker can read any user's password including the administrator's.
real_world_example: Real-world example: GET /my-account?id=administrator — response HTML contains <input type=password value='ADMIN_PASSWORD'>. Read the value from source, log in as administrator.
indicators: Account page contains a password input field pre-filled with the current password
URL contains id= parameter that can be changed to another username
Endpoint /my-account accepts arbitrary id values without ownership check
technique: 1. Log in; access /my-account and inspect page source for password input field
2. Confirm it contains type=password with value= attribute pre-filled
3. Change the id parameter to 'administrator' or other target username
4. Read the password value from the HTML source of the response
5. Log out; log in as administrator with the disclosed password
success_condition: Administrator password is visible in the HTML source when requesting /my-account?id=administrator. Attacker logs in as administrator successfully.
